perturbation.original_text: CGS agrees not to pursue independent business relations with any of TSR's clients for which it provides services for a minimum period of two (2) years from the end date of the program-specific Agreement(s)
perturbation.changed_text: CGS agrees not to pursue independent business relations with any of TSR's clients for which it provides services.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'minimum period of two (2) years' clause effectively eliminates a key non-compete restriction, creating a legal contradiction if the restriction is deemed unreasonably long or broad without a specified end date under applicable state law governing non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements. By removing the 'minimum period of two years' clause, the agreement becomes an indefinite restriction, potentially making it overly broad and therefore unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally invalidates contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a two-year non-compete restriction. The changed text removes this two-year limit, creating an indefinite restriction. According to the provided Law Explanation, this indefinite restriction could be deemed overly broad and unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By removing the time limit, the changed text creates a non-compete that is potentially void under California law, establishing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be promptly returned to TSR upon request or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the Receiving Party return or destroy confidential information, the contract fails to address the proper handling of said information upon termination or request, contradicting typical legal standards for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Omitting the obligation to return or destroy confidential information weakens the measures taken to protect trade secrets, potentially leading to a claim that reasonable efforts were not made to maintain secrecy, thus jeopardizing trade secret status.
perturbation.location: Paragraph after definition of Confidential Information

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, while the changed text removes this obligation. The Perturbation Explanation states this omission contradicts legal standards for trade secret protection because it fails to address the handling of confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that the UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret secrecy, and omitting the return/destruction requirement weakens these efforts. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the combined explanations clearly demonstrate the change weakens trade secret protection, creating a contradiction, as the modified contract doesn't ensure reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that for a period of five (5) years commencing on the date of this Agreement, CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR that is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR that is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: By removing the time limitation (five years), the non-disclosure agreement becomes potentially indefinite, increasing the risk of unenforceability under California law, which requires reasonable time limits for such agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. An indefinite non-disclosure period could be deemed unconscionable because it lacks a reasonable limitation and could unduly restrict CGS's ability to conduct future business, therefore making the contract unenforceable under this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the five-year time limit on the non-disclosure agreement, potentially making it indefinite. This contradicts California Civil Code Section 1670.5, as explained by the LLM, because an indefinite non-disclosure period may be deemed unconscionable due to its lack of reasonable limitation, unduly restricting CGS's ability to conduct future business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1670.5 (a), states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract if it finds any clause to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The omission of a time limit creates the potential for the clause to be deemed unconscionable and therefore unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean any non-public, confidential proprietary information, services to be rendered or transactions contemplated hereby, including without limitation, pricing and material terms and conditions of services offered, intellectual property, enabling software, programming, except that Confidential Information does not include any information that: (i) is required by law to be disclosed (ii) was available to the Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by the Providing Party (iii) was known or becomes generally available to the public or to competitors of the parties hereto other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean any non-public, confidential proprietary information, services to be rendered or transactions contemplated hereby, including without limitation, pricing and material terms and conditions of services offered, intellectual property, enabling software, programming,
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exceptions to the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the agreement now prevents the Receiving Party from disclosing information that it is legally obligated to disclose, that it already possessed, or that is publicly available, which introduces a legal contradiction because a court will not enforce the non-disclosure of information that is already in the public domain or that one is compelled to disclose by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1839(3)(A)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 'improper means' of acquiring trade secrets does not include reverse engineering, independent invention, or any source other than the owner of the trade secret. By removing the exceptions, the agreement could be interpreted as attempting to prevent the Receiving Party from using publicly available information, thus violating the DTSA's allowance for legitimate means of acquiring information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes exceptions to the definition of 'Confidential Information,' such as information required by law to be disclosed or information already publicly available. The changed text removes these exceptions, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this could compel the Receiving Party to not disclose information that it is legally obligated to disclose, which is a legal contradiction. The DTSA allows for acquiring trade secrets through reverse engineering or any source other than the owner. The Law Explanation states the change could be interpreted as preventing the Receiving Party from using publicly available information, violating the DTSA.
